items.q: What is the difference between a silent and a missense mutation?
items.correct: A silent mutation changes a base but the codon still codes for the same amino acid; a missense mutation changes the amino acid encoded.
items.misconception: A silent mutation has no effect because no base actually changes.
items.random_wrong: Missense and silent mutations always produce a non-functional protein.
items.why: Students think 'silent' means 'no change at the DNA level' rather than 'no change at the protein level' (due to genetic code degeneracy).

items.q: What is transported in the phloem of a plant?
items.correct: Sugars (mainly sucrose) and other organic compounds, from sources (e.g. leaves) to sinks (e.g. roots, fruits).
items.misconception: Phloem transports water from roots to leaves.
items.random_wrong: Phloem transports oxygen to all parts of the plant.
items.why: Students mix up xylem (water) and phloem (sugar); they don't link the function with each tissue.

items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist as a less-evolved version.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys but chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: The phrase 'descended from apes' and linear evolution diagrams encourage 'A turned into B' thinking, missing the branching tree model.

items.q: The resting membrane potential is approximately:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: -70 mV. “0 mV” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Glucagon. “Insulin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a neuron has resting potential -70 mV and peak action potential +30 mV, the depolarisation in mV is:
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
